Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty minimal in terms of structured time. I'd usually spend about five hours a week, but it wasn't like a set schedule or anything. I learned that I work better when I read through materials and take notes in my own words, so I'd download stuff and work through it when I could focus. With my anxiety, I found that forcing a rigid routine actually made things worse, so I kept it flexible.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was coming up. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram more intensely, but during quieter weeks I might barely touch anything. I think having that flexibility helped me manage my stress levels, even though it probably looked inconsistent from the outside. I've learned to work with my mental state rather than against it.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or needing to check if there was something new posted that I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline later. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually—I went in with a specific purpose and got out. The notification emails were usually what prompted me to check in.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my daily routine. I treated it more like a resource hub where I'd grab what I needed and then do my actual learning elsewhere. Maybe if I'd logged in more regularly it would've felt different, but with only about six logins, it was more of an occasional tool than a habit.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and documents, then go through them with a highlighter and make my own summaries. The discussion posts were also helpful sometimes because seeing other students' perspectives gave me different angles on the material. Anything I could engage with at my own pace felt valuable.
Q6: I mostly ignored the video content—I watched some, maybe 46 minutes total, but it wasn't my preferred way to learn. I also didn't engage much with the peer interaction features beyond what was necessary. Part of it was my anxiety making group stuff feel overwhelming, and part of it was just that reading worked better for me. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to understanding the material or improving my grade. I also valued anything that let me work independently and at my own pace. If something felt like busywork or required a lot of social interaction without clear benefit, I'd deprioritize it. Basically, efficiency mattered a lot to me given my limited study hours.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on. With my anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, so required work always came first. Optional stuff felt like nice-to-haves that I'd get to if I had mental bandwidth left over, which honestly wasn't often. I don't think I completed many optional activities at all.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and work through them on my laptop without being connected, or I'd print things out and annotate them by hand. I also used external resources like YouTube explanations or other websites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. The LMS was just the starting point, not where the learning actually happened.
Q10: There were many times when I was deep into studying but the LMS wouldn't show anything. Like, I'd spend two hours going through downloaded notes, making flashcards, or rewriting concepts in my own words. None of that shows up anywhere. My exam score of 83 didn't come from nowhere—it came from all that invisible work I was doing outside the system.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd access a reading once, download it, and then spend days working through it offline. The LMS would just show one quick access, but I might have read that document three or four times. This was my main study method—grab the materials and then engage with them in my own space where I felt more comfortable and less anxious.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and engaging felt almost impossible. Sometimes it was triggered by seeing how much I'd fallen behind on task completion, which created this vicious cycle. During those times, I'd pull back from the LMS entirely and just try to manage my mental state. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me develop strategies to push through those moments.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the incomplete tasks made it worse. Fatigue from managing my mental health also meant I had less energy for the course sometimes. I learned to work in short, focused bursts rather than long sessions because that's what my brain could handle.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxious thoughts and I couldn't focus on what I was reading. Those sessions felt wasted even though technically I was 'engaging' with the content. I got better at recognizing when I was in that state and stepping away instead of forcing it.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important—my completion rate shows that clearly. The anxiety made starting things feel huge, like this insurmountable task. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more capable, but sometimes that moment never came. It's something I'm still working on, but my resilience helps me bounce back and at least tackle the most critical stuff.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd log in when something was due soon, grab what I needed, and work intensively to get it done. Without a deadline, I had trouble motivating myself to engage with the platform. It's not ideal, but the pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety paralysis sometimes.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes not in healthy ways. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback could send my anxiety spiraling. I learned to wait until I was in a good headspace before checking grades or comments. When feedback was constructive and specific, it genuinely helped me improve my approach to the next assignment.
Q18: The course structure gave me a lot of flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my anxiety and energy levels, but bad because without external structure, I sometimes drifted. Having 19 online courses to manage meant I had to prioritize ruthlessly. The courses with clearer weekly expectations were easier for me to stay on track with.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement much easier for me since I could study offline. What made it harder was when participation required real-time interaction or frequent logins—that triggered my anxiety. Also, when the course had too many small tasks, I'd get overwhelmed by the sheer number of things to track. Simpler structures with fewer, weightier assignments worked better for my brain.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, downloading things, maybe starting activities but not finishing them. They could be struggling with anxiety or perfectionism, where they engage with the material but can't bring themselves to submit work that doesn't feel good enough. Or they might be figuring out what they need to focus on and abandoning things that seem less important.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My attendance was around 12% and my task completion was low, but I still got a Merit with an 83 on my exam. I was learning effectively offline in ways the system couldn't see. I think some students are just efficient—they figure out what actually matters for understanding the material and focus their limited energy there.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active and which resources you access. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually learning or just clicking through. They can't see offline study, mental effort, or the anxiety someone might be managing while trying to participate. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, and trying to apply what I'm learning. It's a mental state more than a behavior—I can be deeply engaged while sitting quietly with downloaded notes, or completely disengaged while technically logged into the LMS.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my anxiety and how much energy it takes to manage it while studying. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed materials, the concepts I discussed with family, or the external resources I used. It also wouldn't capture my resilience—how I kept coming back and pushing through despite struggling. My Merit grade came from invisible effort that no log could ever record.
